attack_summary: A compromised Ubuntu host (192.168.1.3) running a Sandcat agent was launched by an external attacker to perform an ICMP flood (direct network flood) against a target (7.0.14.254). The agent had elevated privileges and remained live in-network throughout the session.

mitigations.name: Insert inline Snort IPS between FortiGate and internal switch
mitigations.brief_description: Removed the direct FortiGate<->switch link and inserted a pre-configured Snort IDS inline (FortiGate -> Snort -> Switch). The inline IPS blocked the malicious ICMP flood immediately. Caveat: this change initially caused outbound Internet connectivity from the agent to fail (collateral impact) and required follow-up.

LAN Connectivity Check (Ping).description: Checks inter-network connectivity by pinging a target host on the LAN. The target IP is defined by the fact 'lan.target.ip'.

Internet Connectivity Check (HTTP).description: Deadman ability that verifies internet connectivity by making an HTTP HEAD request to a well-known public endpoint. Uses curl instead of ping (ICMP) to test TCP-based network reachability. Returns the HTTP status code.

LAN Connectivity Check (Ping).description: Checks inter-network connectivity by pinging a target host on the LAN. The target IP is defined by the fact 'lan.target.ip'.

Internet Connectivity Check (HTTP).description: Deadman ability that verifies internet connectivity by making an HTTP HEAD request to a well-known public endpoint. Uses curl instead of ping (ICMP) to test TCP-based network reachability. Returns the HTTP status code.

mitigations.name: FortiGate egress/filter rules to deny ICMP from the compromised host and allow HTTP/HTTPS
mitigations.brief_description: Created an address object for 192.168.1.3 and added firewall policies that deny service 'PING' from that host while explicitly allowing HTTP/HTTPS (with NAT) so legitimate web traffic could continue. Validation showed ICMP flood was blocked and outbound HTTP/HTTPS connectivity was restored.

mitigations.name: Host-based iptables rate-limiting and drop rules on Ubuntu agent
mitigations.brief_description: On the compromised UbuntuComputer, inserted OUTPUT-chain iptables rules: a rate-limited ACCEPT for ICMP (approx. 1/s with small burst) followed by a DROP for remaining ICMP. Verified the rules are present; this throttles any locally generated ICMP flood and provides an immediate host-level containment layer.

key_observations: All mitigations respected the live-response constraint: the compromised host remained on the network and the attacker was not removed.
Layered defenses were applied (network IPS, firewall policy, host firewall) and the combined approach reduced attack success rate from 1.0 to 0.0.
Insertion of inline devices can produce unintended path/forwarding side-effects (Snort insertion initially broke outbound connectivity until FortiGate policies were adjusted).

FortiGate policy-based blocking restored legitimate outbound traffic while removing the ICMP attack vector; host iptables provide defense-in-depth but are reversible by a privileged attacker on the host.
Policy scoping and order matter: using 'any' interfaces worked in this scenario but tighter interface scoping (LAN->WAN) and DoS/rate-limit profiles on the firewall are preferable for production.

Logging/monitoring and forensics were enabled on changes (policies configured with logging) but further packet-level logs from FortiGate/Snort and host persistence checks are still needed for investigation.
overall_conclusion: The team successfully stopped the active ICMP flood while the attacker remained in-network by applying a layered defense: inline Snort IPS, targeted FortiGate deny-for-ICMP/allow-HTTPs policies, and host-level iptables rate-limiting. The attack success rate dropped to 0.0 and legitimate connectivity was maintained after firewall adjustments. Recommended next steps: perform forensic investigation and persistence removal on the compromised host, harden/persist host defenses where possible, narrow firewall policy scope and add DoS/rate-limiting profiles, centralize logs/alerts (SIEM), and validate inline device forwarding/health to avoid collateral outages.
